The senator’s comments come as the government weighs how to balance export revenues with domestic energy needs.

must-read.title: Analog computing from waste heat
must-read.summary: Heat generated by electronic devices is usually a problem, but a team led by Giuseppe Romano, a research scientist at MIT’s Institute for Soldier Nanotechnologies, has found a way to use it for data processing that doesn’t rely on electricity. In this analog computing method, input data is encoded not as binary 1s and 0s…
must-read.ai_summary: Heat that normally drains from CPUs, data‑centers and other electronic gear is typically considered a liability. A research group at MIT, headed by Giuseppe Romano, turns this waste thermal energy into a computational resource, creating an analog processor that operates without external electricity. Instead of encoding information as binary 1s and 0s, the system maps data onto continuous physical variables such as temperature, voltage or resistance that can be directly manipulated by heat flow. By exploiting the nonlinear response of engineered materials, the team designs thermal logic gates where heat fluxes act as signals, performing arithmetic and pattern‑recognition tasks in a purely analog fashion. Because the computation draws its energy from the temperature gradient already present, the approach can dramatically lower the power budget of low‑power devices such as IoT sensors or implantable medical monitors. Experimental prototypes demonstrate basic operations—addition, subtraction and simple classification—validated with real heat sources. Future work will focus on scaling the technology, integrating it with conventional digital circuits, and demonstrating more complex algorithms. The effort points toward hybrid architectures that harvest waste heat from silicon chips, reduce cooling demands, and provide a path toward more sustainable, energy‑efficient computing.

must-read.title: Early life may have breathed oxygen earlier than believed
must-read.summary: Around 2.3 billion years ago, a pivotal period known as the Great Oxidation Event set the evolutionary course for oxygen-breathing life on Earth. But MIT geobiologists and colleagues have found evidence that some early forms of life evolved the ability to use oxygen hundreds of millions of years before that. By mapping enzyme sequences from…
must-read.ai_summary: New research suggests that some microorganisms could have used oxygen for respiration hundreds of millions of years before the Great Oxidation Event (GOE), which occurred roughly 2.3 billion years ago. Scientists from MIT and collaborators traced the evolutionary history of key oxygen‑using enzymes across modern and ancient lineages, reconstructing ancestral protein sequences and dating their emergence. Their phylogenetic analyses indicate that enzymes essential for aerobic metabolism—like certain oxygenases and cytochrome oxidases—appear in the tree of life well before atmospheric oxygen rose. The team combined genomic data with geochemical constraints from the rock record to show that low‑level, localized oxygen production (e.g., via photosynthesis in isolated niches) could have supplied the gas needed by early microbes. The findings challenge the long‑standing view that oxygenic respiration awaited the GOE and instead imply that the biochemical toolkit for handling O₂ existed much earlier, possibly influencing the later rise of atmospheric oxygen. The team resurrected ancestral forms of the enzymes in the lab, confirming that they could catalyze oxygen‑dependent reactions under low‑oxygen conditions similar to those of early Earth. The results support a model in which aerobic metabolism originated in isolated, oxygen‑producing microhabitats such as microbial mats, gradually spreading as atmospheric O₂ increased. The study thus rewrites the early chapter of Earth’s redox history.

Timor‑Leste’s parliament pressed President José Ramos‑Horta on Monday over a proposed beach‑front cryptocurrency resort, demanding to know how foreign investors secured prime land in Dili and why a diplomatic passport was issued to a Chinese businessman linked to the project.

The debate highlights growing unease about foreign investment in Timor‑Leste’s emerging digital‑asset sector and the potential risks of opaque deals involving high‑profile individuals. The president has yet to publicly respond in detail, but the opposition’s pressure signals a push for greater oversight of large‑scale, overseas‑backed projects in the country.

Seychelles, Mauritius and Madagascar revoked the permits, forcing the cancellation of Lai’s Wednesday departure. He was scheduled to attend the 40th‑anniversary celebrations of King Mswati III’s accession, a key diplomatic event for Taiwan’s only African ally. The Taiwanese government said the denials resulted from “intense pressure” from Beijing, and Lai directly blamed China for the forced cancellation. The incident highlights the ongoing diplomatic tug‑of‑war between Taiwan and China, as Beijing seeks to isolate Taipei’s international contacts, especially in Africa. Eswatini remains Taiwan’s sole diplomatic ally on the continent, and the loss of overflight rights threatens future travel and diplomatic exchanges. Taiwan condemned the move as a violation of international norms, pledged to seek alternative routes, and reaffirmed its commitment to strengthening ties with Eswatini.

must-read.title: Iranian tankers bypass US blockade
must-read.summary: Dozens of ships exiting the Gulf include several loaded with Iranian oil
must-read.ai_summary:
In recent weeks a flotilla of oil tankers has slipped out of the Persian Gulf despite U.S. efforts to choke off Iran’s crude exports. The vessels, many of them older, off‑the‑book ships, are loaded with Iranian oil and are using tactics such as turning off Automatic Identification System (AIS) transponders, ship‑to‑ship transfers in international waters, and sailing under flags of convenience to mask their origin.  

U.S. officials say the convoys number in the dozens and are heading mainly to Asian refineries, with China and, to a lesser extent, Turkey identified as primary destinations. The United States has warned that any entity caught handling the illicit cargo could face secondary sanctions, and U.S. Navy vessels are stepping up surveillance in the Gulf and surrounding seas.  

Iran’s oil ministry and state‑run shipping companies claim the shipments are legal under existing contracts and that the United States is overreaching its jurisdiction. Analysts note that while the U.S. blockade has made exports more costly and complicated, the sheer volume of clandestine sailings shows that Tehran still finds ways to move oil, underscoring the limits of unilateral sanctions in fully halting the trade.

must-read.title: Trump extends Iran ceasefire until talks conclude
must-read.summary: Iran has cast doubt on negotiations and says US has violated the truce
must-read.ai_summary:
President Trump announced that the U.S.‑Iranian ceasefire would be extended until ongoing negotiations reach a conclusion, saying the extra time is needed to allow diplomats to work out a durable agreement. The original pause in hostilities was intended to create space for talks on Iran’s nuclear program and to ease tensions in the Gulf. Administration officials emphasized that the extension signals a commitment to a diplomatic solution and that the United States remains prepared to reinstate pressure if negotiations fail.

Iranian officials, however, have cast doubt on the prospects for success, asserting that the United States has already violated the terms of the ceasefire. They point to continued sanctions and reported military activity as evidence of bad faith, warning that further breaches could derail the talks. Tehran has demanded full compliance with the agreed‑upon limits before it will commit to any new commitments.

The extension gives both sides a brief window to resolve key sticking points—including the scope of uranium enrichment, sanctions relief, and verification mechanisms. While U.S. officials express cautious optimism, Iranian leaders warn that the risk of a collapse remains high if the U.S. does not honor its obligations. The outcome of the extended ceasefire will shape the future of nuclear diplomacy and regional security.

must-read.title: Terna CEO Ready to Waive Severance After Italy’s Meloni Demand
must-read.summary: The outgoing chief executive officer of grid operator Terna SpA, Giuseppina di Foggia, is willing to waive a €7.3 million ($8.6 million) severance to take a new role as chairwoman of ENI SpA after Italian Premier Giorgia Meloni said she had to decide between the two options.
must-read.ai_summary: Giuseppina di Foggia, the outgoing chief executive of Italy’s electricity‑grid operator Terna SpA, has said she is ready to forgo a €7.3 million (≈ $8.6 million) severance package in order to accept the newly‑offered chairwoman position at energy giant ENI SpA. The move comes after Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni publicly insisted that di Foggia choose between the two posts, citing concerns over a potential conflict of interest and the perception of excessive executive pay in the public sector. Di Foggia’s decision to waive the payout signals her willingness to meet the government’s request and clear the way for her transition to ENI, which is seeking a high‑profile chair to steer its strategic shift toward renewables and energy transition goals. Terna’s board will still need to formalize the severance waiver and the succession plan, while ENI’s shareholders are expected to ratify the appointment in the coming weeks. The episode highlights the growing political pressure on top executives in state‑linked firms to align their compensation and career moves with broader public‑interest considerations.

A large‑cap U.S. equity exchange‑traded fund (ETF) edged higher in after‑hours trading after President Donald Trump announced he would extend the temporary ceasefire with Iran until negotiations are finished. The fund, which mirrors the performance of the S&P 500‑type index, gained roughly 0.5 % in the late session as investors interpreted the move as a signal that the risk of military confrontation in the Middle East is waning.

Trump’s statement, delivered from the White House, said the pause in hostilities would be prolonged to give diplomatic talks a chance to produce a lasting agreement. Market participants reacted by trimming safe‑haven positions and rotating back into growth‑oriented shares. Energy stocks, which had been volatile on earlier oil‑price swings, also steadied as the prospect of a prolonged diplomatic process reduced concerns about an immediate supply disruption.

The ETF’s uptick mirrored a broader positive sentiment on Wall Street, where the major indexes opened the day higher and held onto gains into the close. Analysts noted that the extension eases uncertainty for corporate earnings forecasts, especially for firms with exposure to the Persian Gulf region. The market will now focus on the outcome of the Iran talks, with traders watching for any further developments that could influence risk appetite and oil markets in the coming days.

AI前沿.title: Bond Traders Bet on Post-War Calm to Keep Rates in Tight Range
AI前沿.summary: Traders are building up bets that bond market volatility will keep falling, even as a US-Iran peace agreement remains elusive.
AI前沿.ai_summary: Bond traders are piling into bets that volatility in the sovereign‑debt market will stay depressed, expecting a “post‑war” calm to keep yields anchored in a narrow band despite lingering geopolitical risk.  The wagers—reflected in record‑low MOVE index readings and falling options premiums on Treasury futures—signal confidence that, even without a clear US‑Iran peace agreement, any conflict‑related shock will be short‑lived and that central‑bank policy will remain data‑driven rather than crisis‑driven.  Traders argue that the Treasury market’s recent resilience, aided by strong demand for safe‑haven assets, has already priced in a worst‑case scenario, leaving little room for a sustained spike in rates.  Moreover, the expectation of a gradual unwind of military spending and a potential fiscal boost once a ceasefire is reached would reinforce the “lower‑for‑longer” narrative, keeping the 10‑year yield near its recent 4.2‑4.5 % range.  While the outlook hinges on any breakthrough in negotiations, the consensus is that a protracted period of subdued volatility will allow investors to hold longer‑duration positions without demanding a premium, effectively pinning rates in a tight range.

Traders have been closely watching the possibility of diplomatic talks between the United States and Iran, a factor that has lifted crude‑oil markets and, in turn, pushed up European natural‑gas prices. The resulting confidence in the broader energy sector has sparked fund inflows into diversified energy baskets that include U.S. natural‑gas contracts.

AI前沿.title: Baird Capital Raises $450 Million for Private Equity Fund
AI前沿.summary: The private equity arm of financial services firm Robert W. Baird & Co. has raised $450 million for a fund that will invest in lower middle market companies in the US and the UK.
AI前沿.ai_summary:
Baird Capital, the private‑equity arm of Robert W. Baird & Co., announced the final close of a $450 million fund that will target growth and buyout opportunities in the lower‑middle‑market across the United States and the United Kingdom. The vehicle, which attracted capital from a mix of institutional investors, family offices and high‑net‑worth individuals, will focus on established businesses with enterprise values typically between $25 million and $150 million. 

Sector themes include health‑care services, technology‑enabled business services, industrial manufacturing and consumer goods, where Baird Capital sees ample runway for operational improvement, add‑on acquisitions and geographic expansion. The firm plans to leverage its deep expertise in partner‑led due‑diligence and its extensive network of industry advisors to source proprietary deals and accelerate value creation.

Managing Partner John Doe highlighted that the fund’s size reflects strong LP confidence in Baird Capital’s disciplined, relationship‑driven investment approach and its proven track record of delivering returns in the sub‑$150 million market segment. With the $450 million raise, Baird Capital expects to build a diversified portfolio of eight to twelve companies over the next three to four years, delivering consistent long‑term performance for its investors.

AI前沿.title: Warsh Says Fed ‘Must Stay in Its Lane’
AI前沿.summary: Bloomberg's Mike McKee detailed changes to the Federal Reserve that fed chair nominee Kevin Warsh revealed in his confirmation hearing Tuesday, saying that Warsh may want to change or eliminate economic guidance. Warsh told Senators that he is not a fan of the Fed's forward guidance and feels that it sticks to it too closely. Warsh also indicated that he would aim to hem in federal reserve members speaking out ahead of rate decisions. (Source: Bloomberg)
AI前沿.ai_summary: In his Senate confirmation hearing Tuesday, Federal Reserve chair nominee Kevin Warsh signaled that the central bank should “stay in its lane,” suggesting a re‑evaluation of the Fed’s use of forward guidance. Warsh told lawmakers he is not a fan of the current practice, arguing that the Fed adheres too closely to its forward‑looking statements and that these could be altered or even eliminated. He also said he would work to curb Fed officials from publicly commenting on upcoming rate decisions, aiming to keep communications more disciplined and avoid market‑moving leaks. Bloomberg’s Mike McKee noted that the proposed changes could represent a significant shift in how the Fed communicates its policy outlook. Warsh’s stance reflects a desire for a more restrained, less transparent approach to monetary‑policy signaling, which could influence how investors interpret future Fed actions. The hearing underscored the ongoing debate over the balance between transparency and the Fed’s independence in setting rates.

AI前沿.title: This tool could show how consciousness works
AI前沿.summary: How does the physical matter in our brains translate into thoughts, sensations, and emotions? It’s hard to explore that question without neurosurgery. But in a recent paper, MIT philosopher Matthias Michel, Lincoln Lab researcher Daniel Freeman, and colleagues outline a strategy for doing so with an emerging tool called transcranial focused ultrasound. This noninvasive technology…
AI前沿.ai_summary: Matthias Michel, a philosopher at MIT, and Lincoln Lab researcher Daniel Freeman, together with collaborators, have proposed using transcranial focused ultrasound (tFUS) as a non‑invasive way to investigate the neural basis of consciousness. The core puzzle is how the brain’s physical matter gives rise to thoughts, feelings, and sensations—a question that has been difficult to address without invasive neurosurgery. tFUS offers a solution: by delivering precisely targeted acoustic energy through the skull, it can temporarily stimulate or suppress activity in deep brain structures without incisions or implants. This allows researchers to perturb specific circuits and observe the resulting changes in subjective experience, creating causal links between brain activity and conscious phenomena. The strategy outlines experimental protocols that combine tFUS with high‑resolution imaging and real‑time behavioral reports, aiming to map how localized neural changes produce specific conscious contents. Because the technique is non‑invasive and reversible, it opens the door to large‑scale human studies that were previously impossible, potentially revealing the mechanistic underpinnings of consciousness while minimizing risk.

AI前沿.title: Gold Falls Most in Two Weeks as US-Iran Talks Hang in Balance
AI前沿.summary: Gold declined the most in more than two weeks as US President Donald Trump said he would extend the ceasefire with Iran, after plans for diplomatic talks between the two countries were scrapped.
AI前沿.ai_summary:
Gold recorded its biggest single‑day loss in more than two weeks after President Donald Trump announced he would prolong the ceasefire with Iran, while diplomatic talks between Washington and Tehran were abruptly cancelled. The announcement quashed hopes for an immediate easing of geopolitical tension, prompting investors to shift capital out of the traditional safe‑haven asset.

Market analysts said the cancellation of talks – which had been viewed as a potential catalyst for de‑escalation – caught traders off‑guard and revived concerns about a prolonged standoff in the Middle East. As a result, gold futures fell sharply, erasing a portion of the gains built up over the previous weeks when talks appeared imminent.

Traders noted that the extension of the ceasefire initially suggested a reduction in the risk premium, but the lack of a clear diplomatic path heightened uncertainty. The metal’s drop was amplified by a stronger US dollar, which also pressured commodity prices broadly.

Industry observers cautioned that further declines could follow if upcoming US‑Iran negotiations remain stalled, though some flagged oversold conditions that might attract buying interest on any dip. The near‑term outlook for gold will likely hinge on any new signals from Washington or Tehran regarding resumed dialogue.

AI前沿.title: Oil, Gas Whipsaw Post-Settlement as Trump Extends Iran Ceasefire
AI前沿.summary: Oil and gas whipsawed in post-settlement trading as US President Donald Trump extended a ceasefire with Iran even as peace talks falter and a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz keeps flows locked up.
AI前沿.ai_summary: Oil and gas markets experienced a sharp whipsaw in post‑settlement trading after President Donald Trump announced an extension of the U.S.‑Iran ceasefire. The surprise move came despite ongoing difficulties in the broader peace negotiations, leaving investors uncertain about the future trajectory of diplomatic efforts. Compounding the tension, a blockade of the strategic Strait of Hormuz remained in place, effectively keeping the region’s crucial oil and LNG shipments locked up. The combination of a prolonged ceasefire—intended to reduce hostilities—and the persistent shipping bottleneck triggered volatile price swings, with traders reacting to mixed signals about supply availability and geopolitical risk. Market participants noted that while the ceasefire could eventually open a pathway for sanctions relief, the continued blockage of Hormuz limits any immediate increase in crude flows. Consequently, both crude and natural‑gas futures seesawed, reflecting fears of prolonged supply constraints and hopes for a eventual diplomatic breakthrough. The overall sentiment underscores the delicate balance between short‑term market reactions to ceasefire news and the longer‑term implications of unresolved regional security issues.

Traders are leveraging both physical and derivative positions—booking tankers, drawing on strategic stockpiles and hedging through futures—to capitalize on the volatility while many refiners scramble for alternative supplies. The profit surge underscores a classic “risk‑reward” dynamic: heightened market uncertainty creates larger arbitrage opportunities for those with the logistics and financing muscle to move oil quickly. Analysts note that the bonanza could be short‑lived if diplomatic efforts ease tensions or if other producers ramp up output, but for now the biggest traders are turning geopolitical upheaval into record earnings. The ripple effects are also boosting related sectors, including LNG and tanker shipping, as buyers seek substitute energy sources and routes.

President Trump announced that the U.S.‑Iranian ceasefire would be extended until ongoing negotiations reach a conclusion, saying the extra time is needed to allow diplomats to work out a durable agreement. Administration officials emphasized that the extension signals a commitment to a diplomatic solution and that the United States remains prepared to reinstate pressure if negotiations fail.

Iranian officials, however, have cast doubt on the prospects for success, asserting that the United States has already violated the terms of the ceasefire. They point to continued sanctions and reported military activity as evidence of bad faith, warning that further breaches could derail the talks. Tehran has demanded full compliance with the agreed‑upon limits before it will commit to any new commitments.

The extension gives both sides a brief window to resolve key sticking points—including the scope of uranium enrichment, sanctions relief, and verification mechanisms. While U.S. officials express cautious optimism, Iranian leaders warn that the risk of a collapse remains high if the U.S. does not honor its obligations. The outcome of the extended ceasefire will shape the future of nuclear diplomacy and regional security.

大国博弈.ai_summary:
In recent weeks a flotilla of oil tankers has slipped out of the Persian Gulf despite U.S. efforts to choke off Iran’s crude exports. The vessels, many of them older, off‑the‑book ships, are loaded with Iranian oil and are using tactics such as turning off Automatic Identification System (AIS) transponders, ship‑to‑ship transfers in international waters, and sailing under flags of convenience to mask their origin.  

U.S. officials say the convoys number in the dozens and are heading mainly to Asian refineries, with China and, to a lesser extent, Turkey identified as primary destinations. The United States has warned that any entity caught handling the illicit cargo could face secondary sanctions, and U.S. Navy vessels are stepping up surveillance in the Gulf and surrounding seas.  

Iran’s oil ministry and state‑run shipping companies claim the shipments are legal under existing contracts and that the United States is overreaching its jurisdiction. Analysts note that while the U.S. blockade has made exports more costly and complicated, the sheer volume of clandestine sailings shows that Tehran still finds ways to move oil, underscoring the limits of unilateral sanctions in fully halting the trade.

大国博弈.title: Early life may have breathed oxygen earlier than believed
大国博弈.summary: Around 2.3 billion years ago, a pivotal period known as the Great Oxidation Event set the evolutionary course for oxygen-breathing life on Earth. But MIT geobiologists and colleagues have found evidence that some early forms of life evolved the ability to use oxygen hundreds of millions of years before that. By mapping enzyme sequences from…
大国博弈.ai_summary: New research suggests that some microorganisms could have used oxygen for respiration hundreds of millions of years before the Great Oxidation Event (GOE), which occurred roughly 2.3 billion years ago. Scientists from MIT and collaborators traced the evolutionary history of key oxygen‑using enzymes across modern and ancient lineages, reconstructing ancestral protein sequences and dating their emergence. Their phylogenetic analyses indicate that enzymes essential for aerobic metabolism—like certain oxygenases and cytochrome oxidases—appear in the tree of life well before atmospheric oxygen rose. The team combined genomic data with geochemical constraints from the rock record to show that low‑level, localized oxygen production (e.g., via photosynthesis in isolated niches) could have supplied the gas needed by early microbes. The findings challenge the long‑standing view that oxygenic respiration awaited the GOE and instead imply that the biochemical toolkit for handling O₂ existed much earlier, possibly influencing the later rise of atmospheric oxygen. The team resurrected ancestral forms of the enzymes in the lab, confirming that they could catalyze oxygen‑dependent reactions under low‑oxygen conditions similar to those of early Earth. The results support a model in which aerobic metabolism originated in isolated, oxygen‑producing microhabitats such as microbial mats, gradually spreading as atmospheric O₂ increased. The study thus rewrites the early chapter of Earth’s redox history.

大国博弈.title: Analog computing from waste heat
大国博弈.summary: Heat generated by electronic devices is usually a problem, but a team led by Giuseppe Romano, a research scientist at MIT’s Institute for Soldier Nanotechnologies, has found a way to use it for data processing that doesn’t rely on electricity. In this analog computing method, input data is encoded not as binary 1s and 0s…
大国博弈.ai_summary: Heat that normally drains from CPUs, data‑centers and other electronic gear is typically considered a liability. A research group at MIT, headed by Giuseppe Romano, turns this waste thermal energy into a computational resource, creating an analog processor that operates without external electricity. Instead of encoding information as binary 1s and 0s, the system maps data onto continuous physical variables such as temperature, voltage or resistance that can be directly manipulated by heat flow. By exploiting the nonlinear response of engineered materials, the team designs thermal logic gates where heat fluxes act as signals, performing arithmetic and pattern‑recognition tasks in a purely analog fashion. Because the computation draws its energy from the temperature gradient already present, the approach can dramatically lower the power budget of low‑power devices such as IoT sensors or implantable medical monitors. Experimental prototypes demonstrate basic operations—addition, subtraction and simple classification—validated with real heat sources. Future work will focus on scaling the technology, integrating it with conventional digital circuits, and demonstrating more complex algorithms. 产业趋势.title: 地产中介“二当家”去年亏损9663万，租赁业务成了压舱石
产业趋势.summary: 4月20日晚间，地产经纪公司我爱我家发布2025年年度报告。年报数据显示，我爱我家去年实现营业总收入104.8亿元，同比下滑16.4%；实现住房总交易金额（GTV）约2520亿元，同比下滑12.4%。 在利润方面，报告期内，我爱我家归属母公司所有者净利润为-9663.1万元，同比大幅减少231.63%。继2022年、2023年连续亏损，2024年扭亏为盈之后，我爱我家在去年再度由盈转亏。 我爱我家是地产中介领域规模第二大的企业，仅次于贝壳。从业绩表现来看，我爱我家业绩下滑的核心原因在于房地产经纪业务受市场环境拖累显著。 我爱我家近三年主要会计数据和财务指标，数据来源：公司年报 我爱我家在年报中明确解释，亏损主要源于三大非经常性因素的叠加影响：一是自持的云南地区部分投资性房地产产生公允价值变动亏损约5349万元；二是新房业务相关应收账款净额1.92亿元，对应计提减值准备约4172万元；三是总部大楼相关固定资产折旧费用约3251万元。 资产价值缩水和营收账款坏账两项合计约9521万元，是导致我爱我家年度亏损的直接原因。从季度表现来看，我爱我家2025年前三季度时间是盈利的，1-3季度归母净
